'''Zak Alexander Hardaker''' (born 17 October 1991) is an English professional [[rugby league]] footballer who plays as a {{rlp|ce}} or {{rlp|fb}} for the [[Hull F.C.]] in the [[Super League]]. He has played for the [[England Knights]], [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at international level.

Hardaker previously played for as a {{rlp|ce|fb}} and on the {{rlp|wg}} for [[Featherstone Rovers]] in the [[RFL Championship|Championship]], and the [[Leeds Rhinos]] over two separate spells in the [[Super League]]. He has spent time on loan from [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] at the [[Penrith Panthers]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] and the [[Castleford Tigers]] in the Super League.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

Hardaker has won two [[Challenge Cup]]s and three Super League championships with Leeds. Individually he has been named to the [[Super League Dream Team]] on two occasions, and was the [[2015 Super League|2015]] [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]].

In September 2017, Hardaker failed a drugs test after testing positive for cocaine.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5687 |title=Club Statement: Zak Hardaker |website=Castleford Tigers |date=9 October 2017 |access-date=28 January 2019 |archive-date=29 January 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190129010533/https://www.castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5687 |url-status=dead }}</ref> He was subsequently dropped by Castleford and England and missed the 2017 Super League Grand Final, which Castleford lost 24–6 to Leeds, and the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]] before being dismissed by Castleford on 21 February 2018.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/castleford-tigers/castleford-tigers-dismiss-zak-hardaker-after-failed-drugs-test-1-9028722 |title=Castleford Tigers dismiss Zak Hardaker after failed drugs test |work=[[Yorkshire Evening Post]] |date=21 February 2018 |access-date=21 February 2018}}</ref> On 30 April 2018 [[UK Anti-Doping]] announced that Hardaker was banned from all sports for 14 months, backdated to the date of the original failed drugs test and suspension on 8 September 2017.

Whilst still suspended, Hardaker signed for Wigan in May 2018<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12215/11377359/wigan-warriors-confirm-zak-hardaker-signing-on-four-year-deal |title=Wigan Warriors confirm Zak Hardaker signing on four-year deal |website=Sky Sports |date=18 May 2018 |access-date=28 January 2019}}</ref> before joining them in November 2018 after his suspension ended. Whilst preparing for his comeback, he was banned from driving after being arrested for drink-driving at the end of September 2018.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.wigantoday.net/news/crime/wigan-warriors-star-signing-zak-hardaker-arrested-for-drink-driving-1-9370203 |title=Wigan Warriors star signing Zak Hardaker arrested for drink driving |website=Wigan Today |date=27 September 2018 |access-date=28 January 2019}}</ref>

On 20 January 2019 he made his debut for Wigan, having not played for 16 months, and scored 12 points in their 28-18 pre-season friendly victory over the [[Salford Red Devils]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/new-wigan-warriors-star-zak-hardaker-pleased-to-tick-another-box-1-9547500 |title=New Wigan Warriors star Zak Hardaker pleased to tick another box |date=20 January 2019 |access-date=28 January 2019}}</ref>

On 22 April 2022, it was announced that Hardaker had left Wigan with immediate effect, to return to his family base in Yorkshire, and pursue other opportunities close to home, after he was dropped from the team, for the Easter weekend fixtures against St Helens and Wakefield respectively.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/12595696/zak-hardaker-leaves-wigan-warriors-with-immediate-effect |title=Zak Hardaker leaves Wigan with immediate effect.|work=Sky Sports |date=22 April 2022 |access-date=22 April 2022}}</ref>

On 27 April 2022, Hardaker rejoined Leeds Rhinos until the end of the season, and was selected in the 21 man squad to face Hull KR on 29 April. However, on 28 April, it was reported that Hardaker had collapsed, and suffered a seizure whilst out on a walk with his son, and was taken to hospital in Wakefield as a precaution, before being discharged. Due to health and safety reasons, he did not play.<ref name="Zak Hardaker rejoins Leeds Rhinos">{{Cite news |title=Hardaker's Leeds return delayed after collapse |work=BBC Sport |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/61256123 |access-date=2022-05-02 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

==Background==
Hardaker was born in [[Pontefract]], [[West Yorkshire]], England.

==Playing career==
===Featherstone Rovers===
Hardaker joined [[Featherstone Rovers]] from the local amateur side [[Featherstone Lions]] after leaving [[Carleton Community High School|Carleton High School]] in [[Pontefract]]. He previously played for his hometown club [[Knottingley]] Rockware before signing professional terms with Featherstone Rovers.{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

He scored a hat-trick of tries on his 2010 début for Featherstone Rovers against [[Hunslet R.L.F.C.|Hunslet]], and went on to score 22 tries in 14 games in all competitions in his début season. This included two tries in Featherstone Rovers' extra-time 22–23 defeat by [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] in the [[2010 RFL Championship#Grand Final|Co-operative Grand Final]].

At the end of the season he was named joint Co-operative Championship Young Player of the Year, along with [[Gareth Moore]] of [[Batley Bulldogs]].{{citation needed|date=March 2023}}

===Leeds===
Hardaker signed for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in October 2010 for a transfer fee of around £60,000 and was awarded the number 27 jersey.<ref>{{cite news|title= Leeds sign hottest prospect Zak Hardaker from Featherstone|url= https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/oct/15/zak-hardaker-leeds-featherstone|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|date=15 October 2010|access-date=19 October 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720050935/http://www.guardian.co.uk/sport/2010/oct/15/zak-hardaker-leeds-featherstone|archive-date=20 July 2011|url-status=live}}</ref> He trained with the Rhinos senior squad during pre-season and made two appearances in pre-season friendlies, before returning to [[Featherstone Rovers]] on a dual contract for the 2011 season. He made five further appearances for Featherstone, scoring five tries.

In March 2011, due to injuries to Leeds wingers [[Ben Jones-Bishop]] & [[Lee Smith (rugby)|Lee Smith]], Hardaker was called back to the club & made his [[Super League]] début against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]]. Hardaker scored a try in Leeds' 30–16 loss. Hardaker then featured sporadically for Leeds throughout the season; arguably his best match was in the playoff game against [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]], in which he scored a hat-trick<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/sep/23/huddersfield-giants-leeds-rhinos-super-league|title=Zak Hardaker hat-trick helps Leeds hold off Huddersfield's late charge|newspaper=The Guardian|date=23 September 2011|access-date=10 February 2014|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720050935/http://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/sep/23/huddersfield-giants-leeds-rhinos-super-league|archive-date=20 July 2011}}</ref> and picked up the man of the match award in a 34–28 victory.

He also scored the final try in the [[2011 Super League Grand Final]] as Leeds defeated [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] 32–16 at [[Old Trafford]], Manchester.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-match-report?newsfeed=true|title=Leeds claim Grand Final glory as inspired Rob Burrow sinks St Helens|date=8 October 2011|work=Guardian|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8815944/St-Helens-16-Leeds-32-match-report.html|title=St Helens 16 Leeds 32|date=8 October 2011|work=Daily Telegraph|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref>

Hardaker was handed the number 4 jersey for the 2012 season, taking over from long-time servant [[Keith Senior]]. However, following an injury to [[Brent Webb]], he moved to fullback for the latter half of the season and showed excellent form as Leeds successfully defended their [[Super League]] title. Hardaker was awarded the 2012 Super League Young Player of the Year.

He played in the [[2012 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Warrington Wolves]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/aug/25/leeds-warrington-challenge-cup |title=Warrington's battered Brett Hodgson recovers to see off Leeds in final|date=25 August 2012|work=Guardian UK|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref><ref name="Leeds' Kevin Sinfield stars in Grand Final triumph against Warrington">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/oct/06/warrington-leeds-grand-final|title=Leeds' Kevin Sinfield stars in Grand Final triumph against Warrington|publisher=Guardian|date=6 October 2012|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19362692|title=Leeds Rhinos 18–35 Warrington Wolves|date=25 August 2012|work=BBC Sport|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web
 |url=http://www.warringtonwolves.org/warrington-wolves-are-challenge-cup-winners-2012-5628 
 |title=Warrington Wolves Are Challenge Cup Winners 2012! 
 |date=25 August 2012 
 |work=Warrington Wolves Official Site 
 |access-date=20 November 2019
 |url-status=dead 
 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131217230118/http://www.warringtonwolves.org/warrington-wolves-are-challenge-cup-winners-2012-5628 
 |archive-date=17 December 2013 
 |df=dmy 
}}</ref>

He played in the [[2012 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Grand Final: Warrington 18-26 Leeds|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19805055|access-date=20 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=6 October 2012}}</ref><ref name="Leeds' Kevin Sinfield stars in Grand Final triumph against Warrington"/>

At the start of 2013, Hardaker suffered a broken thumb in a 42–14 victory for Leeds over [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]], causing him to miss the [[2013 World Club Challenge]] match against [[Melbourne Storm]].<ref>{{cite news|url= https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/9878983/Leeds-suffer-setback-as-Zak-Hardaker-is-ruled-out-of-World-Club-Challenge-against-Melbourne-Storm.html|title= Leeds suffer setback as Zak Hardaker is ruled out of World Club Challenge against Melbourne Storm|newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph|The Telegraph]]|date=18 February 2013|access-date=29 October 2013|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720050935/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/9878983/Leeds-suffer-setback-as-Zak-Hardaker-is-ruled-out-of-World-Club-Challenge-against-Melbourne-Storm.html|archive-date=20 July 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url= http://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/leeds-rhinos/rlwc2013-hardaker-bides-time-in-pursuit-of-honours-1-6173563|title=RLWC2013: Hardaker bides time in pursuit of honours|newspaper=[[Yorkshire Evening Post]]|access-date=29 October 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720050935/http://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/leeds-rhinos/rlwc2013-hardaker-bides-time-in-pursuit-of-honours-1-6173563|archive-date=20 July 2011|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url= https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/oct/21/england-australia-tim-sheens-world-cup|author=James Riach|title='Embarrassed' England can still sparkle, says Australia coach Tim Sheens|newspaper=The Guardian|date=21 October 2013|access-date=29 October 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720050935/http://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/oct/21/england-australia-tim-sheens-world-cup|archive-date=20 July 2011|url-status=live}}</ref>

Hardaker was part of the Leeds squad that won the [[2014 Challenge Cup Final]] with victory over [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall’s double stuns Castleford">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|publisher=Guardian|date=23 August 2014|access-date=13 November 2019}}</ref>

He earned his first selection in the [[Super League Dream Team]] at the end of the season. His form also saw him earn a recall to the England squad for the [[2014 Rugby League Four Nations|2014 Four Nations]], however, he did not feature in the tournament.

Hardaker's form continued in 2015, and he was part of the Leeds squad that won the [[2015 Challenge Cup]], with victory over [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Leeds emphatically shut out Hull KR to lift Challenge Cup">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/aug/29/leeds-emphatically-shut-out-hull-kr-to-win-challenge-cup|title=Leeds emphatically shut out Hull KR to lift Challenge Cup|publisher=Guardian|date=29 August 2015|access-date=8 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Scoreboard |newspaper=[[Rugby Leaguer & League Express]] |issue=2982 |date=31 August 2015 |page=31}}</ref><ref name=BBC2908>{{cite web| url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/34086760 |title=Challenge Cup final: Hull KR 0-50 Leeds Rhinos
 |work=BBC Sport |date=29 August 2015 |access-date=29 August 2015}}</ref>

At the end of the season, he was named [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]], the first Leeds player to win the award since [[Iestyn Harris]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/oct/05/zak-hardaker-leeds-man-of-steel-rugby-league|title=Zak Hardaker makes Leeds history by landing the Man of Steel title|first=Aaron|last=Bower|date=5 October 2015|access-date=9 October 2017|website=Theguardian.com}}</ref>

He played in the [[2015 Super League Grand Final]] victory over the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Leeds pip Wigan to seal treble after brilliant, breathless Grand Final">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/oct/10/leeds-wigan-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title=Leeds pip Wigan to seal treble after brilliant, breathless Grand Final|publisher=Guardian|date=10 October 2015|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref>

In June 2016, Hardaker requested a transfer from Leeds following the clubs poor form which saw them sitting bottom of the league table.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/36451429|title=Zak Hardaker: Leeds Rhinos put Man of Steel on transfer list|date=4 June 2016|access-date=9 October 2017|website=Bbc.co.uk}}</ref>

====Penrith (loan)====
On 23 June 2016 Hardaker signed for the NRL team [[Penrith Panthers|Penrith]] on loan for the remainder of the 2016 season in a swap for [[James Segeyaro]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/36600252|title=Leeds Rhinos: Zak Hardaker joins Penrith in swap deal for James Segeyaro|date=22 June 2016|access-date=9 October 2017|website=Bbc.co.uk}}</ref>

He débuted for the Penrith club in Round 17 against the [[West Tigers]] in the Penrith's 26–34 loss. Three weeks later, he scored his first career NRL try in Round 20 in the Penrith's 31–12 win over the [[Brisbane Broncos]] at [[Lang Park]] on 22 July. Zak played with Penrith all the way to the semi-finals where the clubs 2016 NRL season ended. He finished off his maiden year in the [[NRL]] playing in 11 games for the [[Penrith Panthers]] (including two finals games), scoring one try and assisting three others.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nrl.com/telstrapremiership/playerstats/playerprofile/tabid/10898/clubid/10/playerid/5252/seasonid/44/default.aspx|title=Zak Hardaker - Penrith Panthers - NRL.com|website=Nrl.com|access-date=9 October 2017|archive-date=10 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171010004721/http://www.nrl.com/telstrapremiership/playerstats/playerprofile/tabid/10898/clubid/10/playerid/5252/seasonid/44/default.aspx|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===Castleford===
On 8 November 2016, it was announced that he had signed a one-year loan deal to play for [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]], with a deal in place for Castleford to sign him for an "already agreed fee" should all parties agree to it at the end of his loan agreement.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5073|title=Tigers agree Hardaker deal|work=Castleford Tigers|access-date=27 June 2017|archive-date=25 May 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240525022002/https://castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5073|url-status=dead}}</ref>
At the end of the [[Warrington Wolves]] v Castleford Tigers match on the 24 February 2017, Zak donated his man of the match cheque to the Prince of Wales Hospice in [[Pontefract]].

On 27 June 2017, it was announced that he had joined Castleford on a permanent basis, signing a four-and-a-half-year contract with the club.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5545|title=Hardaker is now a Tiger!|work=Castleford Tigers|access-date=27 June 2017|archive-date=21 January 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250121022855/https://castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5545|url-status=dead}}</ref> A transfer fee of £150,000 was agreed between all parties.<ref name=":0" />

His contract was terminated by Castleford on 21 February 2018 following his suspension in September 2017 for drug offences.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/43146986 |title=Zak Hardaker: Castleford Tigers dismiss full-back for failed drugs test |work=[[BBC Sport]] |date=21 February 2018 |access-date=21 February 2018}}</ref>

===Wigan===
On 18 May 2018, it was announced that he had joined [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] on a four-year contract commencing upon completion of his suspension from the sport.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://wiganwarriors.com/news/2018-05-18-warriors-sign-hardaker |title=Warriors sign Hardaker |website=Wigan Warriors |access-date=18 May 2018 |archive-date=18 May 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180518200419/http://wiganwarriors.com/news/2018-05-18-warriors-sign-hardaker |url-status=dead }}</ref> He made his debut for the team in Wigan's pre-season friendly against the [[Salford Red Devils]] on 20 January 2019, scoring 12-points in the 28–18 victory.
[[File:Zak Hardaker Wigan.jpg|thumb|right|Hardaker walking out for the Wigan Warriors at [[Anfield]] in 2019]]
Hardaker played in the [[2020 Super League Grand Final]] for Wigan which the club lost 8–4.  With less than a minute to go in the game with the scores at 4-4, Hardaker had a penalty goal attempt from 40 yards out to win the game for Wigan.  Hardaker missed the conversion and St Helens went down the other end to score the match winning try on the full-time siren.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan 4-8 St Helens|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>

In round 8 of the [[2021 Super League season]], Hardaker was sent off and later suspended for two matches after headbutting [[Catalans Dragons]] player [[Sam Tomkins]] in Wigan's 48-0 loss.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/12321566/zak-hardaker-wigan-warriors-full-back-given-two-match-ban-after-red-card-for-headbutt/|title=Wigan Warriors full-back given two-match ban after red card for headbutt|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>
On 21 April 2022 Hardaker was sacked by Wigan for disciplinary issues.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors-zak-hardaker-set-to-be-sacked-according-to-reports-3663612 |title= Wigan Warriors: Zak Hardaker set to be 'sacked' according to reports |website= www.wigantoday.net|date= 21 April 2022 }}</ref>

===Leeds===
On 26 April 2022, it was announced that Hardaker had rejoined [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] for the remainder of the 2022 Super League season.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12040/12599153/zak-hardaker-rejoins-leeds-after-wigan-exit-its-like-going-back-to-your-old-school/|title=Zak Hardaker rejoins Leeds after Wigan exit - 'It's like going back to your old school'|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>
On 24 September 2022, Hardaker played for Leeds in their 24-12 loss to [[St Helens RFC]] in the [[2022 Super League Grand Final]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/sep/24/st-helens-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report-rugby-league/|title=St Helens sink Leeds to win record fourth consecutive Grand Final|newspaper=The Observer|date=24 September 2022 |last1=Bower |first1=Aaron }}</ref>

===Leigh===
On 20 October 2022, Hardaker signed a contract to join the newly promoted [[Leigh Leopards|Leigh]] side. The move was seen as controversial due to Hardaker leaving Wigan only months earlier citing homesickness and travel time between where the club was located and its proximity to Yorkshire.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/rugby-league-news-live-hardaker-7724611/|title=Rugby League news: Leigh Leopards sign Zak Hardaker after rebrand, Super League transfers|website=www.hulldailymail.co.uk|access-date=21 October 2022|archive-date=20 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221020185045/https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/rugby-league-news-live-hardaker-7724611|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors-fans-react-to-zak-hardakers-move-to-the-newly-rebranded-leigh-leopards-3888329/|title=Wigan Warriors: Fans react to Zak Hardaker's move to the newly rebranded Leigh Leopards|website=www.wigantoday.net|date=20 October 2022 }}</ref>
On 12 August 2023, Hardaker played for Leigh in their 17-16 Challenge Cup final victory over Hull Kingston Rovers.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/66463438|title=Challenge Cup final: Hull KR 16-17 Leigh Leopards - Leigh claim first Wembley final win in 52 years|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>
Hardaker played 23 games for Leigh in the 2023 Super League season as the club finished fifth on the table and qualified for the playoffs. Hardaker played in their elimination playoff loss against Hull Kington Rovers.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://leighleopards.co.uk/leigh-leopards-2023-in-review/#:~:text=Overall%20playing%20record%3A,For%20694%2C%20Against%20580./|title=Leigh Leopards 2023 season in review|website=www.leighleopards.co.uk|date=16 February 2024 }}</ref> In July 2024, [[Hull F.C.]] announced that Hardaker would join the club from the start of the 2025 season on a two-year contract.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/13175201/zak-hardaker-to-join-hull-fc-for-2025-season-from-leigh-leopards/ | title=Hardaker to join Hull FC for 2025 season | work=Sky Sports | access-date=8 July 2024 | date=8 July 2024 }}</ref>
Hardaker played 25 games for Leigh in the 2024 Super League season which saw the club finish fifth on the table. Hardaker played in the clubs semi-final loss to Wigan.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/live-blog/12204/13227859/super-league-semi-finals-live-wigan-face-leigh-for-grand-final-spot/|title=Super League semi-finals: Wigan Warriors cruise past Leigh Leopards- live commentary and video clips|website=www.skysports.com}}</ref>

===Hull F.C.===
On 8 July 2024 it was reported that he had signed for [[Hull F.C.]] in the [[Super League]] on a two-year deal.<ref>{{cite web|website=BBC Sport|date=8 Jul 2024|accessdate=3 Oct 2024|title=Hardaker to join Hull FC from Leigh in 2025|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cxe2v8pyke1o}}</ref>
In round 1 of the 2025 Super League season, Hardaker made his club debut for Hull F.C. in their upset victory over [[Catalans Dragons]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/c20p9z9lvg7o|title=Two tries for Cust as new-look Hull FC beat Catalans|website=www.bbc.co.uk|date=14 February 2025 }}</ref>

==International career==
In 2012, Hardaker made his [[England national rugby league team|England]] début at {{rlp|fb}} at the end of the season in the [[2012 Autumn International Series]] on 27 October 2012 v [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/leeds-18-18-wakefield-zak-hardaker-2962565|title=Leeds 18-18 Wakefield: Zak Hardaker moves on from England nightmare in first appearance since world cup|first=Trevor|last=Baxter|date=26 December 2013|website=Mirror.co.uk|access-date=9 October 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/dates/27-october-2012/wales-vs-england.html|title=Wales 12-80 England|website=www.rugbyleagueproject.org|access-date=9 Dec 2020}}</ref>

Hardaker was named in England's squad for the [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]]; however, he was later forced to withdraw from the squad for personal reasons mid-tournament.

In 2015, Zak made his first appearance for [[England national rugby league team|England]] since the [[2012 Autumn International Series]]. He appeared for England in a test match against France where the English went on to destroy the away team.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/live/match/43791/report|title=England demolish France 84-4 in record win|publisher=Skysports.com|access-date=24 October 2015}}</ref> He was also selected for the [[2015 New Zealand rugby league tour of Great Britain|test series against New Zealand]] which started a week later.

He was selected in squad for the [[2019 Great Britain Lions tour]] of the Southern Hemisphere.<ref name="Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea">{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/14/zak-hardaker-shock-inclusion-24-man-great-britain-squad-tour/|title=Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea|publisher=Telegraph|date=14 October 2019|access-date=29 October 2019}}</ref> He made his [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] test debut in the defeat by Tonga.<ref name="Great Britain left bruised at Tonga party">{{cite web|url=https://www.thetimes.com/article/great-britain-tonga-rugby-league-hzmmhr5q5|title=Great Britain left bruised at Tonga party|publisher=Times|date=26 October 2019|access-date=29 October 2019}}</ref>

*[[Man of Steel Award|Man of Steel]] (1): 2015

==Controversies==
In November 2013, Hardaker withdrew from the [[England national rugby league team|England]] squad during the [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]], citing personal reasons.<ref>{{cite web|title=England's Zak Hardaker pulls out of World Cup over 'personal issues'|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/nov/04/england-zak-hardaker-rugby-league-world-cup|website=The Guardian|access-date=7 January 2018|date=4 November 2013}}</ref> He was later handed a £2,500 fine and a written warning by his club [[Leeds Rhinos]] after he "acted unprofessionally" prior to his withdrawal from the squad.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hardaker hit by Rhinos fine|url=http://en.espn.co.uk/rugbyleague/sport/story/255157.html|publisher=ESPN|access-date=7 January 2018|date=6 November 2013}}</ref>

In June 2014, Hardaker received a five-match ban and a £300 fine for making homophobic comments which appeared to be directed towards referee [[James Child]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Zak Hardaker of Leeds given five-match ban for homophobic abuse|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/jun/10/zak-hardaker-leeds-five-match-ban-homophobic-abuse|website=The Guardian|access-date=7 January 2018|date=10 June 2014}}</ref> Hardaker issued a public apology for the incident, claiming the insult had been directed towards [[Warrington Wolves]] player [[Michael Monaghan]] rather than the referee.<ref>{{cite web|title=Leeds full-back Zak Hardaker apologises for homophobic comments|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/jun/05/zak-hardaker-leeds-rhinos-apology-homophobic-comments|website=The Guardian|access-date=7 January 2018|date=5 June 2014}}</ref>

In March 2015, Hardaker and Leeds teammate [[Elliot Minchella]] were detained and questioned by police over the alleged assault of a 22-year-old student, causing Hardaker to miss his side's 18–6 defeat by Warrington Wolves. Hardaker did not face criminal charges, but admitted to the assault and agreed to pay £200 compensation and write a letter of apology to the victim. He was also once again subject to internal disciplinary action by his club.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/news/latest-news/top-stories/exclusive-leeds-rhinos-players-admit-assault-in-student-flats-1-7155662|title=EXCLUSIVE: Leeds Rhinos players admit assault in student flats|newspaper=Yorkshire Evening Post|access-date=9 October 2017}}</ref>

In October 2017, Hardaker was omitted from [[Castleford Tigers]] squad for the [[2017 Super League Grand Final]] against former club Leeds. Castleford issued a statement saying that Hardaker had been left out for an unspecified "breach of club rules".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/13995/11067805/zak-hardaker-out-of-grand-final-for-breach-of-club-rule|title=Zak Hardaker out of Grand Final for 'breach of club rules'|website=Skysports.com|access-date=5 October 2017}}</ref> On 9 October 2017, Castleford released a statement confirming Hardaker had received a suspension after testing positive for a banned substance.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5687|title=Club Statement: Zak Hardaker'|website=castlefordtigers.com|access-date=9 October 2017|archive-date=29 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190129010533/https://www.castlefordtigers.com/article.php?id=5687|url-status=dead}}</ref> It was also announced that Hardaker would not be selected for the England squad for the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]] after testing positive for cocaine.<ref>{{cite news|title=Zak Hardaker banned from rugby league after positive test for cocaine|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/09/zak-hardaker-failed-drugs-test-rugby-league-england-squad|website=The Guardian|date=9 October 2017 |access-date=9 October 2017 |last1=Bower |first1=Aaron }}</ref><ref name="bbc-cocaine">{{cite news|title=Zak Hardaker: Castleford Tigers full-back banned after positive drugs test|work=BBC Sport |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41542423|access-date=9 October 2017}}</ref>

In September 2018, Hardaker was arrested for allegedly drink driving, driving without insurance, and driving without a valid MOT. He was banned from driving in October 2018, having been found to be over double the legal limit.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-45822780 |title=Zak Hardaker: Wigan Warriors player banned for drink driving |work=BBC News |date=11 October 2018 |access-date=28 January 2019}}</ref>

In February 2024, Hardaker was again found guilty of driving over the prescribed limit of alcohol and driving without due care and attention. He was charged at Leeds Magistrates Court, and received a fine of £1346 along with a three-year driving ban.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/zak-hardaker-drink-driving-banned-28633638.amp |title=Zak Hardaker found guilty of drink driving again with Leigh Leopards star banned |work=Yorkshire Live |access-date=14 February 2024}}</ref> Leigh Leopards issued a club statement, committing their continued support to Hardaker following the incident.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://leighleopards.co.uk/club-statement-zak-hardaker/ |title=CLUB STATEMENT - ZAK HARDAKER|work=Leigh Leopards |access-date=14 February 2024}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/zak-hardaker-drink-driving-banned-28633638/|title=Zak Hardaker found guilty of drink driving again with Leigh Leopards star banned|website=www.examinerlive.co.uk|date=14 February 2024|author1=Yorkshirelive|access-date=15 February 2024|archive-date=15 February 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240215013345/https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/zak-hardaker-drink-driving-banned-28633638|url-status=dead}}</ref>
